1: Nahash the Ammonite besieges Jabesh-Gilead. Jabesh-Gilead had a complex history with Benjamin: it was the one city that refused to join the anti-Benjamin war (Judg 21:8-9), and its daughters became wives for the Benjaminite survivors. Saul's rescue of Jabesh-Gilead thus has a genealogical dimension — he may be rescuing people connected to his own tribe's survival. Nahash means 'serpent'; the name's ominous resonance with Genesis 3 is not lost in a theologically sensitive reading.
2: Nahash's terms are brutal: he will make the treaty only if he may gouge out every man's right eye and bring disgrace on all Israel. The gouging of the right eye — the dominant eye for archery and swordsmanship — would permanently disable the men as soldiers. This is not mere cruelty but systematic military neutralization: a blinded right eye removes the city's capacity for armed resistance. The Ammonite king's confidence in his position is total.
3: The elders of Jabesh buy seven days: 'Leave us alone for seven days so we can send messengers throughout Israel. If there is no one to save us, we will surrender to you.' Nahash agrees — a tactical overconfidence, assuming no one can rally quickly enough. The seven-day window is the narrative opening through which YHWH will act. In a culture without rapid communication, seven days barely suffices for messages to travel and forces to assemble.
4: When the messengers reach Gibeah of Saul, the people weep. Gibeah is Saul's home city — again the Benjaminite geography creates a personal connection. The messengers have come to the right place without knowing it: the man YHWH has anointed to save Israel is in this very city. The people's weeping is the response of those without a deliverer — the extremity of helplessness that YHWH's Spirit will address in v6.
5: Saul comes in from the field behind the oxen and asks: 'What is wrong with the people, why are they weeping?' He is still farming — plowing with oxen is significant peasant-king imagery. Saul has not assumed the royal prerogatives of ch 10; he is still a farmer. When they tell him about Jabesh-Gilead, the Spirit intervenes (v6). Saul's return from the field to receive the Spirit parallels Elisha leaving his plowing (1 Kings 19:19-21).
6: 'The Spirit of God rushed upon Saul when he heard these words, and his anger burned greatly.' The formula ṣālaḥ rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm — the same construction used in the Samson narratives and promised in 10:6 — here combines Spirit-empowerment with righteous indignation (ʾapô bəʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ). The anger is not personal reaction but Spirit-channeled zeal for YHWH's people. The Spirit's coming on Saul at the national crisis confirms the anointing of ch 10 with its first public demonstration.
7: Saul takes a yoke of oxen, cuts them into pieces, and sends the pieces throughout Israel with the message: 'Whoever does not come after Saul and Samuel, so shall it be done to his oxen.' The action echoes Judges 19:29 where the Levite cut the concubine's body into pieces — both invoke the cut-up body as a summons to tribal assembly. Saul's version is an animal, not a human body, but the symbolic grammar is the same. Adding Samuel's name establishes that Saul acts with prophetic authorization, not merely royal command. The fear of YHWH falls on all the people.
8: The muster at Bezek yields 300,000 men of Israel and 30,000 men of Judah — the numbers reflect the idealized tribal levy of a united monarchy, not a standing army. The separate count for Judah hints at the north-south distinction that will fracture the kingdom under Rehoboam. Even in Saul's finest hour, the structural fault lines of Israel's eventual division appear in the census data.
9: The messengers return to Jabesh-Gilead with the news: 'Tomorrow, by the time the sun is hot, you will have deliverance (təšûʿāh).' The word təšûʿāh is YHWH's salvation-word — the same root as Joshua's and Jesus's names. The men of Jabesh rejoice: 'Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you shall do with us whatever seems good to you.' Their surrender-language is now a trust-language; they are no longer surrendering to an enemy but waiting for a deliverer.
10: The men of Jabesh tell Nahash: 'Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you can do with us whatever seems good to you' — the same words to an enemy as in v9 to the deliverer, but with opposite intent. The deception is defensive necessity: they buy the night so Saul can march. The parallel phrasing (kəṭôḇ bəʿêneḵā to both parties) is the narrator's wry double entendre.
11: Saul organizes his forces in three companies for a dawn attack and strikes the Ammonites until 'the heat of the day' — fulfilling the timing promised in v9. The three-column attack (a standard ANE tactical formation) routs the Ammonites so completely that those who survive are scattered 'so that no two of them were left together.' The completeness of the victory underscores the Spirit-empowered deliverance; this is not tactical brilliance but theophanic war.
12: The people ask Samuel: 'Who said Saul should reign over us? Hand those men over so we can kill them.' The worthless men of 10:27 who despised Saul are now subject to popular vindication. The people's demand that the doubters be executed is understandable — the king they despised has just delivered them — but Saul's response is the chapter's moral climax.
13: Saul says: 'Not a man shall be put to death this day, for today YHWH has worked salvation (təšûʿāh) in Israel.' The mercy-on-a-victory-day is remarkable: the day of military triumph, when the king has maximum authority and popular support, is declared a day of clemency rather than vengeance. Saul explicitly grounds this in YHWH's prior act — 'YHWH has worked salvation' — placing the victory outside his own achievement and therefore the right of revenge outside his prerogative. This is Saul at his best.
14: Samuel calls all Israel to Gilgal to 'renew the kingdom' (nəḥaddēš hamməlûḵāh). Gilgal was the first campsite after crossing the Jordan (Josh 4-5) — Israel's original covenant-renewal site in the land. The choice of Gilgal for the renewal anchors the new monarchy in the Conquest traditions. The kingship is 'renewed' rather than newly installed, perhaps indicating that the Mizpah installation of ch 10 was conditional pending a victory demonstration.
15: They make Saul king before YHWH at Gilgal, offer peace offerings (šəlāmîm), and all Israel rejoices greatly. The peace offerings are the covenantal meal that seals the arrangement — after the burnt offering that consecrates the occasion. Saul's kingship is formally installed amid genuine communal celebration, before YHWH, at the most significant covenant site in the land. The chapter ends at the apex of Saul's legitimacy and popular support — the fall of ch 13 will contrast with this height.
